name: Robert Edwin Peary
summary: American Arctic explorer (1856–1920) credited with leading an early expedition to the North Pole; cited in passing in GPB as an emblem of the age's achievements.
importance_reason: One-mention incidental name invoked as a famous contemporary, no role in Bahá'í events.

name: Robert P. Richardson
summary: An exponent of Christian ecclesiasticism (active c. 1915–1916) who wrote against the Faith; listed by Shoghi Effendi among minor Western opponents.
importance_reason: Passing one-mention opponent in a list of clerical critics.

name: Robert Turner
summary: Butler of Mrs. Phoebe Hearst and the first African American to embrace the Bahá'í Faith (c.1855–1909); pilgrim of 1898–99 whom Shoghi Effendi later named one of the nineteen Disciples of 'Abdu'l-Bahá, and whose steadfastness 'Abdu'l-Bahá foretold would make him 'the door through which a whole race would enter the Kingdom.'
importance_reason: First Black Western believer, named a Disciple of 'Abdu'l-Bahá; an oft-taught story despite single GPB mention.
characterizations: Bahaipedia: 'an early American Bahá'í who was honored as the first African American Bahá'í and named a Disciple of 'Abdu'l-Bahá by Shoghi Effendi.'
BahaiTeachings.org: 'Abdu'l-Bahá told Turner that if he remained firm and steadfast until the end, he would be the door through which a whole race would enter the Kingdom.

name: Rusztem Vámbéry
summary: Hungarian jurist and scholar (1872–1948) who left written testimony praising 'Abdu'l-Bahá's 1913 visit to Budapest.
importance_reason: One-mention sympathetic observer who recorded a tribute to 'Abdu'l-Bahá.

name: Ruth White
aliases: the deluded woman (challenger of the Will and Testament)
summary: Early American believer (1869–1942) who became a covenant-breaker, campaigning in the US and England to discredit the authenticity of 'Abdu'l-Bahá's Will and Testament and even petitioning Palestinian authorities to intervene.
importance_reason: A named, recurring-in-memory antagonist whose attack on the Charter is a taught episode, though a single GPB mention.

name: Róbert Nádler
summary: Famous Budapest painter (1858–1938) and leader of the Hungarian Theosophical Society, mentioned in connection with 'Abdu'l-Bahá's visit to Budapest.
importance_reason: One-mention notable contemporary, no role in Bahá'í events.

name: Rúḥu’lláh (martyr of Mázindarán)
summary: A Bábí defender of the fort of Shaykh Ṭabarsí in Mázindarán who was martyred there.
importance_reason: One-mention episode participant among the Ṭabarsí defenders.

name: Rúḥu’lláh (son of Mírzá ‘Alí-Muḥammad Varqá)
summary: Twelve-year-old son of the Hand of the Cause Mírzá 'Alí-Muḥammad Varqá; martyred with his father in the royal prison of Ṭihrán in 1896, strangled with a rope after refusing to recant — a celebrated story of youthful steadfastness.
importance_reason: Child-martyr whose courage is one of the most widely taught Bahá'í stories, though a single GPB citation.
characterizations: Bahaipedia/Bahai Chronicles: Rúḥu'lláh ('the Spirit of God') was referred to as Jináb-i-Mubaligh ('the Honourable Religious Teacher') by Bahá'u'lláh and martyred with his father in the royal prison of Tehran in 1896.

name: Samandar
summary: An early believer of Qazvín whose manuscript history is cited by Nabíl as a source in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: A cited chronicler/source figure with recurring presence in DB footnotes.

name: Samuel Graham Wilson
summary: Presbyterian missionary author (1858–1916) listed among Christian clerics who wrote to oppose the Faith's progress.
importance_reason: Passing one-mention opponent in a list of clerical critics.

name: Samuel Greene Wheeler Benjamin
summary: First United States minister to Persia (1837–1914), cited in The Dawn-Breakers as a Western observer of the Persian court.
importance_reason: One-mention cited foreign observer, no role in events.

name: Sarah (wife of Abraham)
summary: The matriarch and wife of Abraham in the Biblical tradition; named by Shoghi Effendi among the immortal heroines (with Ásíyih, Mary, Fáṭimih and Ṭáhirih) to whom Ṭáhirih is compared.
importance_reason: Scriptural figure of high external standing but a single comparative GPB mention.

name: Sarah Bernhardt (Henriette-Rosine Bernard)
summary: Celebrated French actress (1844–1923) who requested a playwright to write a dramatized version of Ṭáhirih's life.
importance_reason: One-mention famous figure connected only tangentially to a tribute to Ṭáhirih.

name: Sarah Jane Farmer
summary: American spiritual pioneer (1847–1916) who founded the Green Acre conferences in Maine in 1894 and, after her 1900 pilgrimage to 'Akká, placed Green Acre at the service of the Faith she had embraced.
importance_reason: Founder of Green Acre, a recurring institution in Bahá'í history, though a single GPB mention.

name: Sarojini Naidu
summary: Indian poet and independence leader (1879–1949), mentioned in GPB as a notable figure who esteemed the Faith.
importance_reason: One-mention notable contemporary listed among admirers.

name: Sayyid Ameer Ali
aliases: author of The Spirit of Islam
summary: Indian Muslim jurist and historian (1849–1928), author of The Spirit of Islam, cited in GPB among distinguished figures.
importance_reason: One-mention notable contemporary, no role in events.

name: Sayyid ‘Abd al-Raḥmán al-Gaylání (an-Naqíb)
summary: First Prime Minister of Iraq (1841–1927; PM 1920–1922) and Naqíb al-Ashráf of Baghdad who, on instruction of the Council of Ministers, conveyed official sympathy to 'Abdu'l-Bahá's family on his passing.
importance_reason: One-mention statesman who relayed a condolence; notable externally but minor in narrative.

name: Sa‘d-i-Ma‘áḏh
summary: A companion of the Prophet Muḥammad cited in The Dawn-Breakers as one who attained an exalted station through devotion despite being neither of royal blood nor authority.
importance_reason: One-mention illustrative figure from early Islamic history.

name: Sa‘dí of Shíráz
summary: The classical Persian poet of Shíráz, quoted in The Dawn-Breakers for a maxim on the corrupting power of kings.
importance_reason: One-mention literary quotation, no role in events.

name: Sa‘íd-i-Jabbáví
summary: A Bábí native of Baghdád who showed extraordinary courage during the siege of Shaykh Ṭabarsí; shot in the abdomen, he reached Quddús, threw himself at his feet, and expired.
importance_reason: One-mention fort defender with a memorable martyrdom anecdote.

name: Senator George F. Hearst
summary: U.S. Senator and mining magnate (1820–1891), husband of the Bahá'í patroness Phoebe Hearst; mentioned only as her spouse.
importance_reason: One-mention figure identified solely as Phoebe Hearst's husband.

name: Shamsí Big
summary: The Ottoman official appointed to host Bahá'u'lláh in Constantinople; He lodged at Shamsí Big's house and entrusted to him the sealed Tablet to be delivered to 'Alí Páshá.
importance_reason: Two mentions; a named functionary tied to a specific Constantinople-period episode.

name: Shaykh Abú'th-Thaná' Maḥmúd al-Álúsí
summary: Eminent Sunní jurist and Muftí of Baghdád (1802–1854) and renowned Qur'án commentator, in whose home and presence Ṭáhirih defended her faith; depicted in GPB as moved by her.
importance_reason: Recurring (5 mentions) prominent jurist central to the taught Ṭáhirih-in-Baghdád episode.
characterizations: GPB: 'that eminent jurist, Shaykh Maḥmúd-i-Álúsí, the Muftí of Baghdád' in whose home Ṭáhirih defended her faith.

name: Shaykh Ismá‘íl
aliases: Shaykh Ismá‘íl of the Khálidíyyih Order
summary: Leader of the Khálidíyyih Order of Sulaymáníyyih who, during Bahá'u'lláh's Kurdistán retreat, repeatedly entreated Him to relocate to that town; one of the Súfí leaders won by His influence.
importance_reason: Four mentions; a named Kurdistán-period figure tied to Bahá'u'lláh's Sulaymáníyyih sojourn.

name: Shaykh Maḥmúd-i-‘Arrábí
summary: A Muftí of 'Akká once notorious for his bigotry who was converted to the Faith and, in his new zeal, compiled the Muḥammadan traditions concerning 'Akká.
importance_reason: One-mention but memorable convert with a specific anecdote (the bigot turned compiler).

name: Shaykh Muḥammad Muṣṭafá al-Baghdádí
summary: Staunch follower of Bahá'u'lláh, established in Beirut, who safeguarded the interests of pilgrims passing through that city; a notable 19th-century believer.
importance_reason: Frequently appearing (10 mentions) devoted believer who served the pilgrim route, though secondary.

name: Shaykh Muḥammad ‘Abduh
summary: Eminent Egyptian Islamic reformer and Muftí of Egypt (1849–1905) whose several interviews with 'Abdu'l-Bahá enhanced the community's prestige.
importance_reason: Two mentions; externally famous reformer who engaged 'Abdu'l-Bahá, named among signs of the Faith's progress.

name: Shaykh Muḥammad-Báqir-i-Iṣfahání
summary: Leading cleric of Iṣfahán surnamed 'Ḏhi'b' (the Wolf) by Bahá'u'lláh, who pronounced the death sentences of the King of Martyrs and Beloved of Martyrs (1879); addressee of the Lawḥ-i-Burhán and father of the recipient of the Epistle to the Son of the Wolf.
importance_reason: Recurring (6 mentions) archetypal antagonist whose persecutions and the Tablets addressed to him are central teachings.
characterizations: Bahaipedia/bahai-library: Shaykh Muḥammad-Báqir was titled Ḏhi'b ('Wolf') by Bahá'u'lláh; he was among those responsible for the 1879 beheading of the two Iṣfahán Bahá'ís given the titles King of Martyrs and Beloved of Martyrs; the Lawḥ-i-Burhán was addressed to him, and the Epistle to the Son of the Wolf to his son.

name: Shaykh Muḥyi’d-Dín Ibn al-‘Arabí
summary: The great Andalusian Súfí mystic and philosopher (1165–1240), author of the Futúḥát al-Makkíyyah, passages of which Bahá'u'lláh elucidated for Súfí leaders in Kurdistán.
importance_reason: Externally major figure but appears only as the author of a work discussed with Bahá'u'lláh.

name: Shaykh Sulṭán al-Karbilá'í
summary: Father-in-law of Áqáy-i-Kalím and devoted believer who was sent by Bahá'u'lláh's Baghdád friends to find Him during the Kurdistán retreat and beg His return; an important eyewitness whose recollections Nabíl recorded.
importance_reason: Frequently appearing (12 mentions) trusted believer and key Baghdád-period eyewitness source.

name: Shaykh Yúsuf (Muftí of Nazareth)
summary: Learned and esteemed Muftí of Nazareth who accorded 'Abdu'l-Bahá a splendid welcome, sending notables miles to meet Him, and was later received by 'Abdu'l-Bahá in 'Akká.
importance_reason: One-mention dignitary tied to a specific gesture of honor toward 'Abdu'l-Bahá.

name: Shaykh Ṭabarsí
summary: A medieval transmitter of imáms' traditions whose shrine in Mázindarán gave its name to the fort of Shaykh Ṭabarsí, the site of the great Bábí defensive struggle (not himself a Bábí).
importance_reason: One-mention historical eponym of the famous fort, but not an actor in the events.

name: Shaykh ‘Abdu’r-Raḥmán
summary: Undisputed leader of the Qádiríyyih Order, one of the Kurdistán Súfí shaykhs through whom Bahá'u'lláh established contact and to whom He addressed Tablets during His Sulaymáníyyih retreat.
importance_reason: Two mentions; a named Kurdistán-period Súfí leader linked to Bahá'u'lláh's Tablets.

name: Shaykh ‘Alíy-i-Mírí
summary: Muftí of 'Akká who, at 'Abdu'l-Bahá's suggestion, pleaded with Bahá'u'lláh to leave the prison-city at the end of His nine-year confinement within its walls.
importance_reason: One-mention dignitary tied to a specific 'Akká-period episode.

name: Shaykh ‘Uthmán
summary: Leader of the Naqshbandíyyih Order whose adherents reportedly included the Sulṭán himself; one of the Kurdistán Súfí shaykhs influenced by Bahá'u'lláh.
importance_reason: One-mention named Kurdistán-period Súfí leader of note.

name: Sherwin P. Cody
summary: American writer (1868–1959) who described the model of the Mother Temple of the West in the New York Times as embodying 'a Religious League of Nations.'
importance_reason: One-mention journalist quoted on the Temple.

name: Shimr ibn Dhi'l-Jawshan
summary: The man who slew Imám Ḥusayn at Karbalá (d. 686 CE), invoked in GPB as the archetype of cruelty in 'Abbás-Qulí Khán's boast.
importance_reason: Externally infamous figure but used only as a rhetorical archetype of cruelty.

name: Shoghi Effendi Rabbání
aliases: Guardian of the Cause of God
summary: Eldest grandson of 'Abdu'l-Bahá and, by His Will and Testament, the Guardian of the Cause of God (1897–1957; Guardianship 1921–1957); sole authorized interpreter of the Holy Writ, builder of the Administrative Order, and author of God Passes By.
importance_reason: Central figure of the Faith — the appointed Guardian and authoritative interpreter; one of the four foremost figures.
characterizations: GPB: he holds 'the right of the interpretation of the Holy Writ solely conferred upon him' and the Will establishes 'the institution of the Guardianship as a hereditary office.'

name: Sháh-Bahrám Varjávand
summary: The promised World-Savior of Zoroastrian prophecy expected to triumph over Ahriman; in His letters to the Indian believers Bahá'u'lláh proclaimed Himself the fulfillment of this expectation.
importance_reason: Three mentions; a prophesied messianic figure whose fulfillment in Bahá'u'lláh is a taught point, though not a historical person.

name: Siddhártha Gautama (Gautama Buddha)
summary: The founder of Buddhism (traditionally c. 563–483 BC), regarded in Bahá'í teaching as a Manifestation of God; GPB identifies Bahá'u'lláh as the promised 'Buddha named Maitreye' of His prophecy.
importance_reason: A Manifestation of God in Bahá'í belief and a world prophet, though appearing only in prophetic comparison in this corpus.
characterizations: GPB: 'He [Bahá'u'lláh] alone is meant by the prophecy attributed to Gautama Buddha Himself, that a Buddha named Maitreye, the Buddha of universal fellowship should, in the fullness of time, arise and reveal His boundless glory.'

name: Simon Peter
summary: The foremost apostle of Jesus Christ (d. c. 64/67 AD), quoted in GPB for his prophecies of the Day of God and the 'times of restitution of all things.'
importance_reason: Foremost Christian apostle of high external standing, cited for prophecy in this corpus.

name: Sipihr
summary: The Persian court historian (author of the Násikhu't-Tavárikh) cited in The Dawn-Breakers as puzzled by an unprecedented occurrence.
importance_reason: One-mention cited Persian chronicler.

name: Sir Arthur Wigram Money
summary: British soldier (1866–1951) and former Chief Administrator of Palestine who wrote expressing admiration and condolence on 'Abdu'l-Bahá's passing.
importance_reason: One-mention official who sent a condolence.

name: Sir Francis Younghusband
summary: British explorer and writer (1863–1942) whose book 'The Gleam' is cited in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention cited Western author.

name: Sir George Stewart Symes
summary: British colonial administrator (1882–1962), Governor of Phoenicia, who paid a public tribute to 'Abdu'l-Bahá at His funeral, recalling His love for children, flowers, and the poor.
importance_reason: Two mentions; an official whose funeral eulogy of 'Abdu'l-Bahá is quoted.

name: Sir Ignatius Valentine Chirol
summary: British journalist and historian (1852–1929) cited in GPB for his veneration of Ṭáhirih's memory and named among Western figures who attest the Faith's progress.
importance_reason: Four mentions but only as a cited admiring observer of Ṭáhirih.

name: Sir John Malcolm
summary: British soldier-diplomat and historian (1769–1833), author of a History of Persia, cited in The Dawn-Breakers on the Persian court and ministers.
importance_reason: Cited Western historian source, no role in events.

name: Sir Michael Ernest Sadler
summary: British educationist (1861–1943) who presided over a London gathering addressed by 'Abdu'l-Bahá at the Passmore Edwards' Settlement.
importance_reason: Two mentions but only as host/chair of an 'Abdu'l-Bahá address.

name: Sir Patrick Geddes
summary: Scottish biologist and pioneer of town planning (1854–1932), named in GPB among distinguished figures associated with the Faith.
importance_reason: One-mention notable contemporary in a list.

name: Sir Rabindranath Tagore
summary: Indian poet and Nobel laureate (1861–1941), named in GPB among eminent figures who esteemed the Faith.
importance_reason: Two mentions but only as a notable name in a list.

name: Sir Richard and Lady Stapley
summary: British philanthropist Sir Richard Stapley (1842–1920) and his wife, named in GPB among those who received or honored 'Abdu'l-Bahá during His Western travels.
importance_reason: One-mention couple listed among Western contacts.

name: Sir Ronald Henry Amherst Storrs
summary: British administrator and Governor of Jerusalem (1881–1955) who associated with 'Abdu'l-Bahá and wrote movingly of the united regret and respect expressed at His funeral.
importance_reason: Three mentions; an official whose account of 'Abdu'l-Bahá's funeral is quoted.

name: Sir Thomas Barclay
summary: British jurist (1853–1941) who chaired a gathering in Westminster Hall addressed by 'Abdu'l-Bahá.
importance_reason: One-mention chair of an 'Abdu'l-Bahá address.

name: Sir Walter Norris Congreve
summary: British general (1862–1927), Commander-in-Chief of the Egyptian Expeditionary Force, who sent condolences to 'Abdu'l-Bahá's family upon His passing.
importance_reason: One-mention official who sent a condolence.

name: Siyyid Abu’l-Qásim (mujtahid of Zanján)
summary: A mujtahid of Zanján who stabbed a captive with his penknife during the Zanján upheaval.
importance_reason: One-mention episode participant (an attacker) in the Zanján events.

name: Siyyid Abu’l-Qásim-i-‘Aláqih-Band-i-Iṣfahání
summary: A Bábí of Iṣfahán who had accompanied Mírzá Aḥmad and served as a guide/intermediary in the Dawn-Breakers narrative.
importance_reason: One-mention named participant with a minor narrative role.

name: Siyyid Abú-Ṭálib (brother of Siyyid Aḥmad of Sang-Sar)
aliases: Siyyid Abú-Ṭálib, brother of Siyyid Aḥmad-i-Sang-Sárí
summary: Brother of Siyyid Aḥmad-i-Sang-Sárí and a companion among the Sang-Sar believers; safely conducted to Sang-Sar where he survived to live with his brother in Mázindarán.
importance_reason: Four mentions; a named survivor of the Sang-Sar group with recurring presence.

name: Siyyid Abú-Ṭálib (kad-ḵhudá of the Bázár quarter, Nayríz)
summary: Kad-khudá (headman) of the Bázár quarter of Nayríz and a companion of Vaḥíd, whose house was seized and fortified by the enemy during the Nayríz upheaval.
importance_reason: One-mention named local figure in the Nayríz events.

name: Siyyid Abú-Ṭálib-i-S̱hahmírzádí
summary: A believer of Shahmírzád whose written account of the Mázindarán struggle, sent to the Holy Land, was a source for Nabíl's narrative.
importance_reason: One-mention cited source for the Mázindarán account.

name: Siyyid As̱hraf-i-Zanjání
summary: A Bábí of Zanján beheaded as he held the body of his martyred companion; remembered with his mother, who urged him to remain steadfast and refused to mourn had he recanted — a celebrated martyrdom story.
importance_reason: Four mentions; martyr whose story (with his heroic mother) is widely taught.
characterizations: GPB: he was 'so brutally beaten that blood flowed from under his nails, was beheaded, as he held in his arms the body of his martyred companion.'

name: Siyyid Aḥmad (martyr of Qazvín)
summary: A Bábí martyr of Qazvín named in passing in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: Bare one-mention name in a martyr context.

name: Siyyid Aḥmad (son of Vaḥíd)
summary: A son of Vaḥíd (Siyyid Yaḥyá-yi-Dárábí), mentioned as accompanying his father in the Dawn-Breakers narrative.
importance_reason: One-mention family member of Vaḥíd.

name: Siyyid Aḥmad-i-Sang-Sarí
aliases: Siyyid Aḥmad, brother of Siyyid Abú-Ṭálib-i-Sang-Sárí
summary: A noted divine and companion of Quddús from Sang-Sar, son of the learned Mír Muḥammad-‘Alí; brought before the prince and martyred, his body cut to pieces by Mírzá Muḥammad-Taqí and the seven 'ulamás of Sárí.
importance_reason: Eight mentions; a learned martyr of the Mázindarán episode with recurring presence.

name: Siyyid Aḥmad-i-Yazdí
aliases: Siyyid Aḥmad, father of Siyyid Ḥusayn-i-‘Azíz
summary: Father of Siyyid Ḥusayn-i-‘Azíz, the amanuensis of the Báb; a Bábí mentioned in The Dawn-Breakers chiefly through that relationship.
importance_reason: Five mentions; identified mainly as the father of the Báb's amanuensis.

name: Siyyid Aḥmad-i-Zanjání
summary: A Bábí resident of Zanján named among the believers of that city in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention Zanján believer listed without distinguishing detail.

name: Siyyid Baṣír
summary: Blind Bábí of India, wealthy and learned, who lost his sight at seven yet perceived the truth of the Cause with his inner eye; likely the same person as 'Siyyid Baṣír-i-Hindí' below.
importance_reason: A memorable blind Indian believer whose conversion story is taught; appears under two near-identical entries.
characterizations: Bahaipedia: 'Siyyid Basir-i-Hindí (d. 1851) was an early Bábí... born in Multan, India, into a family descended from the Prophet Muhammad; at seven a severe case of chicken pox blinded him.'

name: Siyyid Baṣír-i-Hindí
summary: Blind Indian Bábí (d. 1851), introduced to the Faith by the Letter of the Living Sa'íd Hindí; though sightless he immediately perceived the message's significance, and was martyred at Tabríz on Íldirím Mírzá's order, his tongue drawn out through the back of his neck.
importance_reason: Memorable blind teacher-martyr with a widely retold conversion and martyrdom; five mentions.
characterizations: Bahaipedia: introduced to the religion by the Letter of the Living Sa'íd Hindí; after converting he taught the Bábí Faith across Iran and Iraq until being executed in 1851.

name: Siyyid Dáwúdí
summary: A Sunní notable of Baghdád who, with Shaykh 'Abdu's-Salám and Shaykh 'Abdu'l-Qádir, sought Bahá'u'lláh's presence and enrolled among His earliest admirers in the city.
importance_reason: One-mention Baghdád-period admirer of Bahá'u'lláh.

name: Siyyid Ibráhím-i-Qazvíní
summary: A powerful and notorious Shí'ah leader who incited the enemies of Siyyid Káẓim-i-Rashtí against him, stirring up sedition in Qazvín.
importance_reason: Two mentions; a named antagonist of the Shaykhí leader.

name: Siyyid Ibráhím-i-Ḵhalíl
summary: A Bábí of Tabríz entrusted with the safekeeping of the Báb's Tablets and commentaries; a recurring custodian figure in the Dawn-Breakers narrative.
importance_reason: Three mentions; a trusted custodian of the Báb's writings in Tabríz.

name: Siyyid Ismá'íl-i-Zavári'í
aliases: King of Martyrs and Beloved (Bahá'u'lláh's praise)
summary: A native of Zavárih and formerly noted divine, surnamed Ḏhabíḥ ('the Sacrifice'); severed from all earthly ties, his self-appointed task was to sweep the approaches of Bahá'u'lláh's house in Baghdád, until he took his own life — Bahá'u'lláh extolled him as a martyr of supreme purity.
importance_reason: Frequently appearing (12 mentions) immortalized lover of Bahá'u'lláh whose story of devotion and self-sacrifice is widely taught.
characterizations: GPB: 'one of His ardent lovers... surnamed Ḏhabíḥ (the Sacrifice), formerly a noted divine, taciturn, meditative and wholly severed from every earthly tie, whose self-appointed task... was to sweep the approaches of the house in which Bahá'u'lláh was dwelling.'
Bahaipedia/Bahai Chronicles: Bahá'u'lláh extolled him as 'King and Beloved of Martyrs,' stating that no blood had been poured upon the earth as pure as the blood he shed.

name: Siyyid Ismá‘íl (son of Vaḥíd)
summary: A son of Vaḥíd (Siyyid Yaḥyá-yi-Dárábí), left in his mother's care as mentioned in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention family member of Vaḥíd.

name: Siyyid Jamálu’d-Dín-i-Afg̱hání
summary: Pan-Islamic agitator and ideologue (1838–1897), an originator of the constitutional movement, whose relentless hostility and powerful influence were gravely detrimental to the Faith's progress in Near Eastern countries; GPB notes he died of cancer after a checkered career.
importance_reason: Externally famous, recurring (3 mentions across both books) antagonist whose enmity affected the Faith's spread.
characterizations: GPB: 'The scheming Jamálu’d-Dín Afg̱hání, whose relentless hostility and powerful influence had been so gravely detrimental to the progress of the Faith in Near Eastern countries.'

name: Siyyid Javád (son of Ḥájí Mírzá Siyyid ‘Alí)
common_name: Siyyid Javád (son of Ḥájí Mírzá Siyyid ‘Alí)
summary: Infant son of Ḥájí Mírzá Siyyid 'Alí (the Báb's maternal uncle, a Martyr of Ṭihrán) who died in infancy, noted in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention infant who died young; purely genealogical note.

name: Siyyid Ja‘far, Kas̱hfí
summary: One of the greatest 'ulamás of his period, surnamed Kashfí ('the Discloser') for his skill in interpreting the Qur'án and revealing divine secrets; father of Vaḥíd (Siyyid Yaḥyá-yi-Dárábí).
importance_reason: Two mentions; a celebrated divine, notable chiefly as Vaḥíd's father.

name: Siyyid Ja‘far-i-Yazdí
summary: Once-influential and honored Bábí who suffered public humiliation for the Cause — his turban befouled and burned, paraded door to door to beg — a memorable persecution episode in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: Four mentions; a named believer with a vivid, recurring persecution anecdote.

name: Siyyid Káẓim (merchant of Zanján)
summary: A noted merchant of Zanján who attained the Báb's presence in Shíráz and accompanied Him to Iṣfahán; brother of Siyyid Murtaḍá, one of the Seven Martyrs of Ṭihrán.
importance_reason: One-mention believer notable for early companionship of the Báb and his martyr brother.

name: Siyyid Káẓim-i-Rashtí
summary: Leader of the Shaykhí school (c. 1793–1843) and successor of Shaykh Aḥmad-i-Aḥsá'í; teacher of Quddús and of Ṭáhirih (whom he surnamed Qurratu'l-‘Ayn), who on his deathbed sent his disciples to seek the promised Qá'im — a direct prelude to the Báb's declaration.
importance_reason: Foremost herald-teacher whose Shaykhí school and dying charge launched the search that found the Báb; by far the most-mentioned (102) figure in this slice.
characterizations: GPB: 'Siyyid Káẓim, the teacher of Quddús and of Ṭáhirih (whom he surnamed Qurratu'l-‘Ayn), leader of the Shaykhí school.'

Wikipedia/Bahaipedia: 'before dying, instead of appointing a successor, he sent his disciples out to find the Promised One'; he died 31 December 1843, and some Shaykhís became Bábís and later Bahá'ís.

name: Siyyid Káẓim-i-Zanjání
summary: A Bábí who accompanied the Báb on His journey to Iṣfahán in September 1846.
importance_reason: Six mentions; a companion of the Báb on a specific journey, but limited narrative role.

name: Siyyid Mihdí (son of Vaḥíd)
summary: A son of Vaḥíd (Siyyid Yaḥyá-yi-Dárábí) who accompanied his father, mentioned in passing in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-mention family member of Vaḥíd.
